speaker: interviewer
text: When you were taken to the ghetto, this was the first time you had been directly confronted by Nazis as opposed to Hungarians.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, when they took us in the ghetto. Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, number one, it was a horrible, horrible fear when you saw them, even. They were spic and span in their boots and terrifying, absolutely shivering, shivering. So, you know, we already knew so much about Nazis and all that because we were aware of everything what was going on in the world and what Hitler has done, so horrible, horrible fear, and also seeing people being beaten because they, unfortunately, had nervous breakdowns. They didn't know they were yelling, and they were screaming. And they, they-- so instead of-- but eventually, they were taken away. I don't know what happened to them. And questions, you never asked. You know, there were whispers and things, but you didn't ask any questions. Anybody didn't dare. So then also the Nazis started to give up everything that you have there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any awareness of what was happening to the Jews in eastern Europe up until then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, because my father came home, and he told us. I remember the words-- you know, those were all very foreign words-- that the earth is moving, that some people in the Ukraines were, were-- dug their own graves and thrown in. And some were still alive, and they were shoved with the earth. So all those stories were terribly, terribly terrifying to us. They were terrifying even at home because you were attacked. And if, if a cat ran by the house or a, or a bird or something, you always feared, they're breaking in. All those things, it was a horrible, horrible fear on all of us. So-- but we were-- we knew that the Nazis, what they were doing in, in [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Poland because Poland, they started persecuting them much earlier than us. And also in, also deep in Czechoslovakia because my father had a brother that lives, lived in, in Czechoslovakia. And they came home. And they said how the Jews were rounded up and how they were beaten and all that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So only, you know, as being a kid here, and, and they didn't want the child should listen in on those things. Yet, you still-- you were aware of what was going on. So that part, and the ghetto, as I said, we were taken out-- we were already the third transport. We were taken to the trains, again, to the cattle cars that took us to Auschwitz. And you were with your whole family still?

speaker: interviewee
text: The whole family. The cattle car was a horrible, horrible thing also. They shoved us in there without much food-- without any food, without any water, without any [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] bathroom facilities. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Everybody had just enough room almost to stand, not even to sit because there wasn't even enough. You kept on changing off. One would sit down. One would stand up. And men, women, children screaming. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] People when they're in such a close confinement, they're on each other. So it was, it was a horrible-- they did put in a barrel of water. And if you had to use the bathroom, you had to take a sheet and tie it around somewhere. And it was a horrible experience. There was only those, a very small cubicle that air came in. So I remember [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I had just turned 18, and I was looking out how free the world is-- [CRYING] sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: But those were the last few days that I was with the family. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And as we arrived in Auschwitz-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] excuse me-- [CRYING] [DRINKS] after several days-- I don't know, maybe five days-- we arrived in Auschwitz. And [SIGHS] we saw that there were a lot of Nazis by the, by the train. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And also, the prison uniforms, those were the Jewish boys, the Jewish men. And as we got out, they announced it, a loudspeaker, nobody was to take anything out from the cattle cars. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And everything, everything yet will get to your destination. Don't worry about it. And mothers and the children on one side, and younger men on the other side, and young, healthy-looking men, women on one side. We were all with my mother, with all the children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know where you were?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] No, but then Jewish, Jewish fellows that were working there came over to me and grabbed me by the arm. And he says, you go in that group, and pinch your cheeks. I guess I was-- we were extremely already undernourished. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And my sister followed me. My mother only shouted, take care. [CRYING] Sorry. [CRYING] And that was the last goodbye. [CRYING] My mother and the four children with her friend with her. My father went separately with the young, with the men, with the working men. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] they said they're going to take the mothers and the children on trucks, and we will march. We will walk.

speaker: interviewee
text: So Mengele was there. That's the first time we saw Mengele, and he was the one that was going through and left, right, left, right. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So they finally rounded us up, the working ones. And I have never seen my mother, whether she went on trucks or walked or killed or whatever. And they took us to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: As we were marching, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we saw that there were camps with lights blaring and people shouting in different languages, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you have bread, throwed it. You have bread. Throw food over. If you have food, throw it over.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we said, oh, must be a mental institution because they were-- heads shaved off and very-- dressed very weird, dressed, you know, like mental people. So we didn't know what they were, why they were shouting. So first, we didn't have anything because we were not allowed to.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I said, that's, that's impossible. And then we went to Auschwitz where they told us to strip naked, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [SIGHS] leave the clothes where you are and move over. We were completely naked. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And while we were standing, the Nazis came. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [BACKGROUND NOISE] And there were some attractive, young, good-looking girls. They were pulling them out, sex purposes. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Then we were-- our heads were shaved off, and they said, they're going to-- you're going to be deloused and taking showers. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] So that's what I did. After [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we took the showers and shaved off, we were going through a line that they gave us one piece of clothing to put on. We didn't care, you know. And those were already Jewish girls that were working there. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] You had to see if it was short or you changed, maybe, to another girl that she had a very long one. I was tall. I had a hard time because I was still also-- they gave me something that it was very short, changed it with somebody for a little longer. And then they marched us [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to, to the, to the-- it was called, the camp, the Lager in German, the Lager. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I couldn't find my sister already because it was even hard to recognize anybody because the heads were shaved off. All you had to do is yell, scream. It was at night, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and they brought us into the barracks. We were in-- it was called Lager [GERMAN], C in English, C-Lager, in the block. The barracks that we stayed was 20, 22, number 22. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They, they were the, the sort of beds that housed about 1,000 women. This put us in 10 to a bed with all you, all you were able to do is lay on one side. And at night, when everybody, everybody had to turn one way because there wasn't enough room, and it was a three-story. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] I know the bathroom, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the bathrooms, you were not allowed to go at night. So there were young girls up there screaming, they had to go to the bathroom. They wouldn't allow them, so they urinated on the ones that were in the bottom. So we  were all initiated, urinated on from the upper ones. In the morning, somehow didn't sleep, but [INAUDIBLE]. You started thinking, I'm missing everything. I missing my parents. I'm missing, I'm missing my sister. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But I found my sister. My sister-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the difference between my sister and myself was always that she had stamina, aggressiveness in her. I was the opposite. I found her, and she was always watching out for me. I was the  older one, but she was watching out for me. She was always like a shadow behind me, watching over me. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And then this is-- we asked the Blockfuhrer, it was called-- the girl that, you know, this happened to be a Czech girl which had been in the camp already for a long time-- where, where is the rest of the family? Where, where are my parents? She says, did you see those big, tall chimneys outside? Those big, tall-- she said. Yes. Did you see the smoke? We could smell, you know, like, human flesh burning. She says, those were your parents. So I said, she's so mean and such a misery. And she tells us. Yet, she's, she's lying. It's all-- we cried, terribly. She's lying. Why is she being so mean to us? But those were the facts. So one day while we were in Auschwitz, we, we saw a band marching. Because this was a, a women's camp that we were in, but for some reason we saw-- I don't even remember this how-- the, the men were marching and somebody shouting, anybody from Bilky? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My sisters and I said, yeah. Who are you?

speaker: interviewee
text: We recognized the father because my father wasn't that much different. He couldn't recognize us because we've-- heads shaved off and in rags. But we recognized him.

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, is your brother with you? I said, no. Where's your brother? Didn't you stay with your mother? No, and they said, [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we stayed in Auschwitz [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] from May until, I would say, the beginning of September. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Was the routine was getting up 4 o'clock in the morning. And it was called zahlappell. For some reason, they were fanatics about counting us so that anybody didn't escape. And if one was missing, then we had to knee with our hands up for hours until that human being was found, whether dead or alive, because there were always deaths, but they had to account for it. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] One day, [INAUDIBLE] she was a rabbi's wife. So she got [INAUDIBLE] because we didn't have any, we didn't have any utensils. We had absolutely nothing on us.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she found a piece of rag and put it on her head [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] because an Orthodox woman is not allowed to be bareheaded. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So they would beat her. She should take it off.

speaker: interviewee
text: She said, no. It's God's will. So we begged her. Take it off because they're going to kill you. And every day was the same thing until they beat her to death in the front of us and beating other souls, so constantly the beating. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] And this was the daily routine. And then during the day, we were either-- if it was-- if there were no air raids, then we were allowed to be outside the barracks. But if there were air raids, they had tightly locked in. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] once in-- yeah, excuse me, yeah, the food, we, when we woke up, 4 o'clock, there was always, they selected a few to go pick up the food for us. We were getting like a, a soup. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So bring it in the barracks, and they would give it to us in a, one bowl. And everybody shared. Everybody had to drink out of that bowl. And if there was left, then it went around again. And a little water with some grass in it. We could feel the sand under it. And then we developed some kind of a disease that's in your mouth from-- it must have been a contagious thing. So they would come and infect our mouth with something. And they gave us in the food also that none of the women had periods. This, this was how it was in the food. Their aim was also [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to destroy to see that even if anybody survived, they shouldn't reproduce. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They did not succeed. They did not succeed. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So some of the women suffered a great deal, you know. Their faces got blown up and blotchy, and their legs got blown up. And their whole bodies were blown up from the normal things that the normal human being has to not having the period.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So this was the daily routine in Auschwitz. And every-- we were, were taken for delousing, you know, to-- and especially that was somewhere in Birkenau, I believe it was. We never knew the names of the places. They never told us. They just marched us there [INAUDIBLE] and then stripped that piece of rag that you had on and, and put it in, in to delouse it because we were full with lice.

speaker: interviewee
text: It didn't matter if you kept to yourself or you went to shower, but if your next-- the person next to you didn't, so you-- a couple hours later, you had it again.

speaker: interviewee
text: So there were no, no warm showers or anything that you ran into. You were allowed to go in in the morning, ice cold, from the faucet and wash yourself as you could. And this is about, you know, outdoor toilets that you were, you were sitting, also, nothing but toilets.

speaker: interviewee
text: One day, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we knew that there, there were only rumors on the next bed to us, that a woman was pregnant because they never allowed it, you know, they never-- if you were pregnant or something. And during the night, we could hear a muffled scream because she gave birth. And they knew that if they'll know that she gave birth, the Nazis, they'll immediately kill her, so they suffocated the baby. They made her-- dragged her out in the morning to go to the-- to be counted, zahlappell, it was called. And the baby was-- oh, this was a horror thing because right next to us, it left a horror impression. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] She loved that baby. Later life, I used to say, I'm not going to have children. I don't want ever my children should experience what I experienced. [CRYING] And this is how it went on. They would come-- when a transport would come in, they would put on the lights. And the lights, if you went five, six feets from the light-- you know, we were surrounded with, like, a barbed, a huge barbed wire with lights. And a transport came in, and they were going to march. They put on the lights that in order-- if you went five, six, feet from the wires, you were electrocuted. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] One day I decided [CRYING] I had-- I didn't want to live anymore. And I'll go to the wires and make an end. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My shadow was right behind me.

speaker: interviewee
text: She [INAUDIBLE] me, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] why are you going so close? Why-- don't you know what's going to happen? [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] So I had to, had to go on. I had to abandon that idea.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But the selections, they would come and pull out anybody that, you know, they would-- the Nazis, the women, they were horrible, absolutely horrible. They would just walk through you and look at your face. And if they didn't like the way you looked out, they knew where you were going. And they were selecting always for work and for, and for the ovens. We were never selected, not my sister, not myself, because we were not, you know, we were, we were under-developed really and very frail looking. So-- but life in Auschwitz was one horror, one horror, horrible nightmare. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] The selections, there one day-- there was a camp that the Czech families-- this was-- the Czech families were mostly-- they were taken early. They were taken in '41, '42, and they were together. First, they were in Theresienstadt, and then they were sent to Auschwitz, but [INAUDIBLE] whole families. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So on the next to-- on the other-- like on the other block was the Czech camp. They called it the Czech camp because the families were together.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a typhus broke out there. So the, the Nazis only told us, don't go to that-- close to there, because it's the contagious, it's a contagious disease. And sometimes they would throw over a little bread [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] or a piece of clothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- Auschwitz was a miserable, miserable weather. Either it was very hot that you would faint, or it was bitter cold. And you know, we didn't-- we were not-- we didn't have, so we would hold on to each other and our bodies would-- one would warm the other.

speaker: interviewee
text: So one day we heard screaming during the night. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They burned down the whole camp because typhus had spread, so they burned it down.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And, and the food, the food in Auschwitz, it was, it was-- in the morning, it was that little soup that they gave us. And in the afternoon, again, it was the counting. Then after the counting, they would give us a piece of bread with margarine, with a piece of margarine, and that was-- those were the rations for the day.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were just biting that hard piece. It was like a brick, the size of a brick cut in, in four or six, whatever, and everybody had a, a slice of it. Occasionally, men would come in to clean the, the toilets, and that's how they, we would send out messages and, sometimes, receive a message. And we would say, Bilky, do you know if there is any people from Bilky? Next time I'll come, I'll ask. So they, they would go and inquire and bring back a message, this one, and this one, you know, this one, and this one. We never had any messages from any-- from my father or anything.
